data.title: SFL: Is The Above-10% Yield Worth Chasing Post-Cut?
data.description:
My previous update on SFL (NYSE:SFL) was all the way back in December of 2023, when I argued that its fat yield looked durable because the company’s long-dated backlog insulated cash flows and left room for further dividend hikes.

That hypothesis aged well, as management pushed the payout from $0.25 to $0.26 in Q4-23 and then to $0.27 through 2024 and into Q1-25. However, I’m circling back now because that trend reversed, with SFL trimming the dividend to $0.20 in August. The cut arrived alongside soft Q2 results, as Hercules was idle, while several vessel sales have reduced near-term cash generation. The company appears to want more balance-sheet flexibility while refreshing its fleet.

What Changed And Why The Cut Happened

Looking at SFL’s Q2 results, it's pretty easy to see why the recent cut took place. Its charter hire billed was about $194 million, with about 87% coming from shipping and 13% from energy (i.e., the rigs). Adjusted EBITDA from consolidated subsidiaries was about $104 million, plus $8 million from equity associates, and net income squeaked in at a mere $1.5 million ($0.01/sh).

The pressure point was the semisub rig Hercules. After contracts in Namibia and Canada in 2024, the rig returned to Norway and has been warm-stacked because of uncertainty in the industry and oil-price volatility, which delayed new employment. That idleness clearly hurts, and the company is absorbing stacking costs while it hunts for work or “strategic opportunities” (whatever that means, and it certainly doesn't spur confidence).

Meanwhile, SFL has been selling and redelivering older ships, which is a typical strategy in the shipping industry to renew one's fleet and is helpful for quickly raising cash and upgrading efficiency. In Q2, for instance, it sold the 2005-built 1,700 teu container vessel Asian Ace. But simultaneously, these sales trimmed recurring charter income, which also contributed to the dividend cut.

How The Dividend Case Looks Today

At today’s $0.20 quarterly payout, the dividend yield stands at a hefty 10.3% and costs the company about $27 million per quarter, or just under $108 million per year. Consensus estimates project EBITDA of nearly $400 million this year, which is expected to continue covering the dividend. The thing is that even when looking at next year's (2026) or even the year after (2027) consensus EBITDA estimates, they hover below last year's $540.5 million, due to the reasons I just discussed.

Moreover, the company should pay about $175 million in interest expenses from out of EBITDA this year, so the coverage isn't that wide, especially when you consider any other outflows below this line. But overall, I think that today's levels should be more or less sustainable from here.

With Hercules idle, I’m not betting on energy earnings to contribute much here. However, the shipping book is heavily reliant on fixed charters, and the company continues to phase out older tonnage, so the post-cut dividend appears serviceable from shipping alone at the current run-rate, barring a step-down in charter hire (which is unlikely given its long-term nature) or a spike in operating costs.

Valuation Check: Cheap on Book, Not on Cash Flow

On price-to-book, SFL seems inexpensive, with the multiple standing at about 1.0. That’s before giving credit to the value of contracts (i.e., backlog written at rates not fully captured by depreciated book).

However, on forward cash-flow multiples, things look less forgiving, with the forward EV/EBITDA having climbed into the mid-9x area that is hardly bargain territory for a leveraged lessor with an idle rig. This reflects how the forward figure has crept up from its LTM 7.6x, as future estimates have softened.

Does The Valuation Reflect Today's Risks?

Now, the problem I have is that today's somewhat rich, in my view, valuation may not be taking into account the underlying risks. The Hercules employment issue, for instance, could become quite severe for the company if no changes are made soon. The rig has been warm-stacked in Norway since late 2024, and until a real contract appears, it will remain a drag.

In the meantime, SFL's ongoing fleet renewal could drain cash flow if those vessels that are on short-term charters, like their dry bulk vessels, end up renewing at lower rates once the current contracts expire. Admittedly, dry bulk rates are doing ok now, but adverse macro factors could easily send them lower.

And lastly, as I mentioned earlier, SFL services have rather high interest expenses, and with its net debt to EBITDA climbing higher recently, another dividend cut could potentially take place if pressure endures, even if the current fleet employment does alone cover payouts.

Where I Land

Frankly, SLF looks somewhat more shaky since my last update almost two years ago. I still like the setup of the business, including its fixed charters with reputable lessees and high fleet utilization, while the new payout should be more defendable compared to the $0.27 quarterly one. The dividend yield is also quite high and well-above 10%.

However, it also reflects the underlying risks, like the Hercules employment, the ongoing fleet renewal, and the rising leverage. In the meantime, the mid-9x forward EV/EBITDA isn't low enough to justify chasing the stock, so I don't think the risk-reward mix makes much sense today.

If you choose to go long, pairing your shares with covered calls could help further boost the income and protect against further downside, all while still targeting great annualized returns given the underlying dividend and income from the premiums.

data.title: Graphite Miners News For The Month Of September 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the September edition of the graphite miners news.

The past month saw China battery-related flake graphite spot prices slightly lower.

Graphite price news

During the past 30 days, the China graphite flake-194 EXW spot price was down 0.97%. The China graphite flake +195 EXW spot price was down 1.73%. Note that 94-97% is considered best suited for use in batteries; it is then upgraded to 99.9% purity to make “spherical” graphite used in Li-ion batteries. The spherical graphite 99.95% min EXW China price was down 1.44% the past 30 days. Note: Graphite electrode prices all moved higher.

Note: SMM lists the current flake-194 graphite spot price at US$369/t including VAT, slightly lower than US$378 last month.

Graphite demand and supply forecast charts

The IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2024 report forecasts graphite demand and supply pipeline to 2040 (source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

2021 IEA forecast growth in demand for selected minerals from clean energy technologies by scenario, 2040 relative to 2020 - Increases Of Lithium 13x to 42x, Graphite 8x to 25x, Cobalt 6x to 21x, Nickel 7x to 19x, Manganese 3x to 8x, Rare Earths 3x to 7x, And Copper 2x to 3x

Graphite market news

In mid-September, Fastmarkets reported:

Graphite: Rising battery graphite prices amid demand growth and geopolitical tensions...Battery graphite prices have been making consistent gains in the last two months amid indications of strong growth in demand from the Chinese active anode sector and the focus that these markets have in US and Chinese trade tensions. Overall market participants are hopeful that the traditionally slow summer seasons starting to end will lead to increased liquidity in the markets but the overall abundance in global inventories will remain a weight on prices.

On September 16, Bloomberg reported:

US in talks to set up $5 billion fund for critical mineral deals.

On September 24, Interesting Engineering reported:

China pledges 7–10 percent emissions cut by 2035, sixfold boost in renewables. World leaders are unveiling new climate targets ahead of COP30. Xi pledged a 7–10% emissions cut, while the EU floated a 66–72% reduction goal.

Note: Bold emphasis by the author. Renewables typically need battery energy stationary storage ("BESS") and therefore battery metals.

data.title: Yes, AI Is A Bubble - Why Nvidia Will Still Be The Last One Standing
data.description:
About a week ago, I think it was Thursday, I woke up and opened my Seeking Alpha app, which is usually my morning routine, and a giant flashing orange ticker tape was staring at my face

"Breaking News: Nvidia, Intel sign blockbuster deal to co-develop chips; Nvidia to take $5B stake in Intel."

I was like, what?

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) investing in Intel (INTC)? The same Intel that's been getting its lunch eaten by Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). It was surprising for many of us, including me. I mean, who could have seen this one coming out of nowhere? And then, like three days later, we got the news that Nvidia will invest up to $100B to build AI data centers with OpenAI. One hundred billion. At this point I, think AI is turning into a bubble.

Just a funny thing the other day on X (formerly Twitter), I saw a meme that was basically saying, "At this point these companies are just saying big numbers and seeing what happens."

But here's the thing , guys: the biggest beneficiary of this bubble would be Nvidia, after all. Because these big tech companies are announcing hundreds of billions of dollars in CAPEX for data centers, most of the high margin GPU revenue would go to Nvidia. Because they have 94% market share in the GPU market.

About a month ago I wrote a piece about Nvidia where I said that growth would eventually slow down and the expected growth is already priced in. But what happened in the last week or so was NOT priced in. And now I am re-rating it to Buy.

Because I believe these two deals plus the hyperscalers CAPEX that is growing at a crazy rate would make them a $10 trillion giant by 2030. I am not kidding. This can happen. Here is how.

Nvidia Just Created A New Chip Segment

Let me walk you through why these deals just supercharged Nvidia's entire growth trajectory. I thought Nvidia's growth was slowing down sequentially, so they might see their parabolic growth decelerate in 2026. But nope! That is not the case here. It's a rocket ship.

If you see Nvidia investing $5 billion in Intel and think it's just a strategic investment or maybe a hedge against TSMC, it's not a bad way to see it, but I think that Nvidia is basically buying its way into markets that were previously closed to them.

Right now, the x86 server CPU market is worth about $50 billion annually. And Intel owns roughly 70% of this market, with AMD taking most of the rest.

Nvidia has exactly zero percent of this market. But here's where it gets interesting. The deal isn't just about Nvidia getting Intel to manufacture chips; I think it's about making custom x86 CPUs optimized for Nvidia's AI platforms.

In almost all data center deployments, customers buy CPUs from Intel or AMD, and then they buy GPUs from Nvidia separately.

There's inefficiency in this model because these components aren't optimized for each other and there's latency in communication between chips. But with this Intel partnership, Nvidia can now offer integrated CPU-GPU solutions where the x86 processor is specifically designed to work with their GPUs. The performance improvements alone could be 30 to 40%.

Now why does this matter financially? Because Nvidia can get a much larger share of the data center wallet. Instead of just selling a $30k H100 GPU, they can sell a complete chip that includes the optimized CPU. Their addressable market per server would just increase by 50%. When you're talking about millions of servers being deployed globally, that's hundreds of billions in additional TAM.

The genius part is that Nvidia doesn't even need to compete directly with Intel or AMD in the CPU market. They are creating a new category of AI-optimized CPU-GPU packages. It's like when Apple made the iPhone. They didn't compete with Nokia and BlackBerry; they made a new thing altogether. And we all know how that turned out.

The PC Market Play

Intel sells roughly 250 million processors for PCs annually. That's their installed base. Now, with this deal Intel will build x86 system-on-chips that integrate Nvidia's RTX GPU chiplets directly onto the processor package. Right now, most of the PC chips have integrated GPU cores inside them, which is not enough for the upcoming workload that will be run on PCs and we will need dedicated GPUs for it.

Right now, if you want a gaming PC or a workstation with good graphics, you buy an Intel CPU for maybe $300 and a separate Nvidia graphics card for anywhere from $500 to $2k. So, two separate chips taking up space and power in your system. But with integrated RTX chiplets on Intel processors, suddenly every Intel processor could have Nvidia graphics built in. Even if Nvidia only gets $100 per integrated chiplet, which is far less for a graphics card, multiply this by potentially 100 million units per year, this could be $10 billion in completely new revenue. And this is extremely high margin revenue, because they are just licensing the design to Intel, not manufacturing anything themselves. Honestly, I don't think the market has even begun to price this in.

Because the enterprise PC segment is due for a massive upgrade wave in 2026. The companies usually refresh PCs every 4 years, and the last big wave was during COVID in 2021. And this time these companies will want AI capable machines. And guess what? The Intel Nvidia integrated chips will be the obvious choice. Even if Nvidia gets revenue from just 30% of these 200 million units that are sold annually at $100 per unit, that's $6 billion in annual revenue just from PC refreshes.

The OpenAI Deal

Now the OpenAI deal is a completely different beast financially. Nvidia is investing up to $100 billion, but they are not just buying equity; they are basically building infrastructure that will generate recurring revenue for at least 10 years.

OpenAI has said that they want at least 10GW of Nvidia systems. But this time they are not selling hardware to OpenAI just like they sell to others. They will likely be providing infrastructure as a service, or, as I like to call it, (IaaS).

Just like AWS, OpenAI will pay for compute capacity over time rather than buying hardware upfront. This will add a new business model, recurring subscription revenue, to their financials. This will smooth out their revenue curve and make it much more predictable. Wall Street loves predictable and recurring revenue; just look at the valuations of SaaS companies compared to hardware companies.

If Nvidia invests $100 billion in infrastructure and charges a 30% gross margin for compute services (which is conservative compared to AWS's 60%+ margins), you are looking at $30 billion in annual gross profit once fully deployed. At a 20x multiple (again conservative for a subscription business), it gives us a $600 billion market cap from this single partnership.

We Might Be In A Bubble

Hold on, just bear with me for a second. I am gonna say something that might sound contradictory, but stick with me here.

We are absolutely, undeniably 100% in an AI bubble. As I was saying earlier, "at this point these companies are just saying big numbers and seeing what happens." OpenAI needs $300 billion here, Microsoft (MSFT) is about to spend $120 billion there, Meta Platforms (META) casually drops $600 billion like it's nothing. It's gotten to the point where anything like $10 billion doesn't even make headlines anymore.

But here's the thing: knowing that we are in a bubble doesn't mean you should run for the exits. Some of the best investment opportunities in history came from riding bubbles before they burst or shorting them right before they burst. The key is knowing the mechanics of why this bubble will keep inflating for years before it pops, and then riding with the companies that will benefit most till the music stops.

And there's no company better positioned for this than Nvidia. Because they are not only selling the shovels in this gold rush, but selling shovels that rust and break and need to be replaced every few years.

Here's something that some of you may or may not know, and this is why I think we're in a bubble, but a bubble that will continue to grow for a very long time. These AI data centers that are currently being built are depreciating assets. Not its book value, its actual competitive value. Why? Because Nvidia releases new chips every 18-24 months that are roughly 2.5x more powerful for the same price. The older data center value basically depreciates by about 15 to 20% every year because new, better chips with more efficiency are launched every two to three years.

On average, an AI data center has a lifecycle of 7 years. Why seven? Let's say Oracle just built a data center worth about $10 billion and started renting it. After seven years, a new center with more performance and lower cost will be built on new chips. Most customers would want that computer instead of the old ones.

By the time that happens, it would be about 2032, and then we would have overcapacity of compute, but it would be obsolete compute. Then people will realize what just happened. But until then, we have plenty of time.

Here's how the cycle may play out. In its first 2 years, the data center is cutting edge and would operate at 90% utilization. By year 4 it will still be competitive, but newer facilities with better chips are coming online. Pricing pressure will start, and utilization will drop to 60%. By year six you are behind the curve and mostly serving price-sensitive customers or those locked into contracts.

And year 7, which would be the end of life. The cost to operate (power, cooling, maintenance) would exceed the revenue you can generate. It's time to shut down or completely rebuild.

By 2032 we will have this massive capacity of old data centers all hitting the end of life simultaneously. But by then we will also have built 2027 and 2028 vintage data centers. Each generation usually is 2x more powerful than the last. By then we will have so much compute capacity that prices will crash, and suddenly everyone will realize we have built 5x more infrastructure than we actually need just to beat each other in this AI race.

That's when the music stops. That's when you will see massive write-downs and all the usual end-of-bubble fireworks. But this is maybe 7 years away from right now, or maybe who knows how long.

But the important part is 7 years of Nvidia selling into this frenzy. 7 years of compounding revenues. And that’s why I think Nvidia would be the biggest beneficiary of this bubble because of its market share and because it's selling something that loses its value over time. So, you can imagine hundreds of billions in depreciating assets that big tech is buying. What would that result in? Continuous refresh cycles and continuous revenue for Nvidia.

Now let's talk numbers to quantify this whole thing. McKinsey said that this AI race would cost us about $7 trillion over the next five years. And $4.2 trillion of it would go toward GPUs, CPUs, server racks, etc.

Keep in mind that this does not include the $300B deal OpenAI made with Oracle, the $100B deal between OpenAI and Nvidia, and the $600 billion investment commitment by Meta. So in total, about $5.2 trillion would go towards data centers alone, which is mainly GPUs and networking equipment. This spend would happen over the next five years. And I have a feeling we are not done with the new spending announcements.

Let me break down where this money will go, because this is where it gets interesting for Nvidia. Of that $4.2 trillion, that will go to the data center, but actually with the new deals it's more like $5.2 trillion. The rest of 2 trillion will go to land, building construction, power infrastructure, cooling systems, but that’s not Nvidia's business.

Now within that $5.2 trillion data center spend about 70% cost is the GPUs. So 70% of $5.2 trillion equals $3.64 trillion in GPU spending over five years.

Nvidia has 94% market share in AI training GPUs. Even if I assume they lose some share, let's say they drop to 92% to be conservative, it is still $3.35 trillion. Let's round down to $3.3 trillion to be extra conservative. Spread over five years, that's $660 billion per year.

Not gonna lie, when I first calculated this, I thought I must have made an error. I mean Nvidia's total revenue last year was $130 billion. So we are talking about them potentially quadrupling their revenue by 2027. Now you can go ahead and discount this revenue by another 30%. It's still $487 billion. And on a P/E basis of 20x (which is conservative for a company growing this fast), this yields us about $9.7 trillion in valuation.

Think about it from a hyperscaler's perspective. You are Amazon, and you have built $50 billion worth of AI infrastructure. Microsoft builds a newer data center with Nvidia's latest chips that are 2.5x faster for the same price. Now Microsoft is giving out the same AI services for 40% less than you, or they can give 2.5x the performance for the same price. What do you do? You have no choice but to upgrade. This creates an AI infrastructure treadmill. Once you're on it, you can't get off. You have to keep running just to stay in place. And every step on that treadmill puts money in Nvidia's pocket.

In normal tech infrastructure, you might refresh every 7 years, and even then it's often optional. You can squeeze extra life out of old servers if you need to. But with AI, falling behind by even one generation puts you at such a disadvantage that customers will literally leave for your competitors. And that’s why I think Nvidia can be a $10 trillion company by 2030.

Right now we are in the phase where the smart money knows it's a bubble but also knows there's money to be made. The hyperscalers have no choice but to play. They can't afford to be the one company without AI infrastructure. It's mutually assured destruction if they don't keep up with competitors.

data.title: Tomra: I Still See An Upside Below 159 NOK/Share After Q2 2025
data.description:
Tomra Systems ASA (OTCPK:TMRAY) is a company I have been investing in for years, trying to get the company's shares at a compelling price while selling off during peak valuation. Tomra is one of those companies that I believe has a timeless appeal and also one that warrants a significant premium of at least 28-33x P/E over time. The market has at times given the company premiums of 50-60x P/E, justified by significant rates of organic growth.

Over the past 20 years, the company's rate of EPS growth has averaged about 14%—less than some, but higher than most companies. Furthermore, on a forward basis, the company is now expected to average about 20% per year or higher.

A positive thesis here is based on continued growth from expansion to new countries, as well as improving legislation and demands for recycling, which affects Tomra positively in turn. A negative set of expectations would be, in turn, expected based on lower margins, lower volumes, or competition from existing or new entrants.

One of the company's primary challenges has been the slowdown in growth in its core segments, like reverse vending. The company's strategy to offset this has been to develop adjacent opportunities in things like fruit and vegetable sorting and other areas.

For this article, we have the 2Q25 period, which we'll look at for the 2025-2027E returns. My latest article for the company can be found here.

My position in the company is at about 0.4% at this time, slightly up over time.

Tomra—Upside from 2Q25 and beyond

Tomra's fundamentals are solid. With an EBITA margin of 18% or above (target), a ROCE of over 15-16%, and a dividend payout of 40-60% of EPS, safeguarded by an investment-grade credit rating, the company is not in any sort of fundamental danger or risk.

The company today is very different from its original state in 1972, when the company invented the first Reverse Vending Machine. Since then, the company has moved into Tomra Food (aforementioned sorting), Recycling, and very recently, the "Horizon" segment, which can be characterized as the company's most forward-looking ventures.

Looking at revenue and dividend growth, you can see the company achieving good results for at the very least the past 10 years of operating history.

Anyone arguing against the company's fundamental appeal is arguing against drivers for circularity and resource optimization, which, given the state of the world today, would be a hard argument to make. Between things like decarbonization, regulation, modernization, and efficiency, as well as demographics, continued urbanization, environmentals, and growing resource scarcity—all of these things speak in favor, not against a company like Tomra.

Even if parts of the company's operations are perhaps a bit more volatile (especially food), over time, I believe in the company's own targets of double-digit growth.

Further legislation, pushes in Decarbonization across the world, and ongoing modernizations alone are expected to result in almost double-digit growth per year until 2030E.

Recent 2Q25 results were mixed. Every single segment except the food segment (typically volatile, now seeing significant growth) was down. Collection, the company's core segment, was down 12% in revenues, while food was up 15%. Gross margin levels came to about 44%, which was more or less in line, and operating expenses are within expectations, slightly down from last year. EPS was mostly in line with previous-year levels for 2Q25.

The trends to keep an eye on here are a significant decline in Recycling order intake—down 37%—while down 37%, while Food sorting systems instead are seeing over 20% order growth on a YoY basis.

On a 1H25 basis, things don't look much better either. A slight increase of about 1% in group-wide revenues, annualizing at about €1.3B per year, with confirmed weakness in recycling/collection, while food is currently driving results upwards. This doesn't trickle down to liquidity, which continues to be good at almost €100M of unused credit lines.

Europe continues to be one of the company's most important areas. This has both to do with geographical proximity and focus, but also with legislation, which is affected by EU standards. In Poland, the new legislation mandates a deposit on plastic bottles up to 3 liters, reusable glass up to 1.5 liters, and 1-liter metal cans and below—all driving benefits to Tomra, which is set to launch new deposit return systems in the country.

Similar trends and legislations are driven in Greece, in Portugal, in Spain, in Moldova, in the UK (new law in January of 2025), and in other European countries. In the international arena, things are a bit slower. In NA, focus is on Canada, with the province of Quebec—the company is set to place over 1,350 machines in the next few years. Australia, meanwhile, launched its new deposit system about 4 months ago.

The company has to manage multiple geographies and legislative areas at the same time in both recycling and collection. While trends right now aren't as positive, it's made up for by a quicker growth in food sorting orders.

Given that the food segment for some time was a poor-return and poor-demand sort of area, it's interesting to see just what has resulted in this improvement for the segment. It has to do with inflation, labour, and macro trends. As customers face increasing labour costs and increasing quality and safety requirements, the mathematics of using automated food sorting and safety systems is changing, and this is what is driving the company's better results. Tomra now fully expects high single-digit annual growth rates over the cycle towards 2030, with a 15% EBITA margin target or above. Based on an increasing order backlog, the company expects a conversion ratio of about 55% for the next quarter.

I look with some interest towards the company's Horizon plans as well. The company has recently announced two investments. First, Tomra is investing in advanced feedstock sorting plants for post-consumer plastic recycling, with plants expected to be operational in 2025-2026.

The company also very recently went ahead and bought a "bag drop" solutions company called C&C Consolidated Holdings, which operates under the CLYNK brand. This method involves consumers dropping off entire bags of empty containers and having them refunded. This sort of technology is a good complement to its existing reverse vending technology, and these have potential, especially in the NA region. Tomra paid about $45M for the company, which for the company in question represented a sub-10x EV/EBITDA—so while not necessarily cheap, Tomra at the very least did not overpay for the M&A.

Tomra's trading history is characterized by significant valuation spikes, with the most severe one during ZIRP. I went ahead and bought when the company dropped in 2022-2023, adding slowly over time. While I could have sold successfully at least 2 times since, I have elected not to, believing instead that significant earnings expansion is part of the company's realistic potential in a period of 3-5 years.

2Q25 does not bring this closer in an obvious or realistic way. Instead, the quarter seems to be one that confirms the company's current challenges. Tomra has an excellent position in collection and recycling, but increasing demands for more complex and advanced solutions in these markets increase the demand for the company's investments and products. Things like sorting aluminum and magnesium or other metals and materials with different densities require sensor innovation and advancements in imaging technology.

While the company's fundamental drivers—the macro ones—remain in line, the future will be one where Tomra will be expected to innovate even more than it already is.

For valuation, it requires that we not adhere to the massive premia that the company has at times traded at.

Tomra Valuation—Upside of 15% annualized is still there, but I am looking at potentially lowering my PT.

The past 20 years have seen Tomra at lows of below 13x P/E and at highs above 80x P/E, and those highs have not even been accompanied by something like a 100%+ EPS growth rate. The patterns here have been hard to explain by anything other than exuberance, and it's why looking at Tomra over the long term requires great care. The 5-year P/E is 54x, the 10 year P/E is 40x, and the 20-year P/E comes to around 32x.

My own estimates go below even that. As detailed in previous articles, I prefer a range of 28-32x P/E for this company, ignoring essentially only the very lowest valuation multiple ranges, while ignoring most of the ones over 33x P/E as exaggerated for the long term.

Current EPS growth rate forecasts call for a 14% growth this year, followed by 30-40% growth in 2026-2027E, though the forecast accuracy for this is not great historically, so I'd expect closer to half this. The current average weighted P/E is close to 36x. It's not the cheapest the company has been in 10 years, but it's cheaper than many times over the past 4-5 years.

More importantly, using any of the 26-32x P/E forecast range here, we get at the very least a market-beating rate of return based on current EPS growth estimates. Even at the very lowest 26x P/E forecast, it's still over 17% per year.

And at the higher 32x P/E, that annualized rate of return goes all the way up to 29% per year.

To stay conservative, I believe that Tomra is worth around 220-230 NOK in terms of fair value for the native ticker. To get a 15-20% annualized upside towards this fair value target, you should buy the company at below 160 NOK—especially since the company's yield, even at this cheap price, is no higher than 1.38% (though that yield is well-covered).

My previous PT for the company was 159 NOK—I retain that PT as of this article and as of 2Q25. While the quarter wasn't the most impressive, I believe the longer-term trends for the company are supportive of a significantly higher value. I believe the recent surge in orders and interest in the food sorting segment confirms this.

If you want to invest in recycling and this sort of technology but not buy Tomra, your choices are limited. Peers to Tomra are limited to companies such as Ishitva Robotic, Sortera Technologies, and AMP, not all of which are even publicly traded. Because of the limited availability of peers and of the general appeal of Tomra, I don't see other companies presenting a compelling investment thesis here—Tomra is the company you should go for if you're interested in the segment.

My 159 PT for the native comes to a TMRAY ADR PT of $16.1/share, making it a "BUY" for the ADR as well.

Risks to such an investment are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Whenever you invest in a company that trades at such a premium, there are risks involved in terms of overpaying and volatile ups and downs. We can see examples of this in the company's historical trading patterns, with significant movements in share price levels in a short time. Beyond those, I believe other risks, including things like evolving regulations, can be offset by the company's positioning in recycling and collecting and where Tomra can "rise beyond" whatever challenges exist and may present themselves.

There may come a time when the company also needs to seriously scale up operations in a way that its current market cap and its current leverage/credit availability don't easily allow for—that's something to keep in mind as well.

Beyond that, the thesis for Tomra is as follows.

Thesis

  • Tomra is a market-leading, world-leading reverse vending machine and recycling business. At the right price, it becomes a “Must Buy” with a holding target that goes beyond the usual for a superb business model with proven reach and scalability. It’s one of the most solid future recycling/reverse recycling plays out there.
  • Tomra remains a play on valuation more than anything else, and because it’s so highly valued, that assumes a very high growth rate. While this is currently expected, I’m more dubious about the exact rate and volume of growth here. This influences how I view the valuation of Tomra.
  • Anything above 35-40x P/E is, in my view, a no-go for this business, and I want it cheaper. I don't believe sub-25x P/E is realistic, but 25-32x is the most I will pay, and I am still able and willing to pay this here, putting it at around sub-160 NOK.
  • This comes to, and remains as of Q2'25, a PT of around 159 NOK conservatively, and this makes the company a “Buy” here, if very barely one. I'm close to downgrading Tomra here if it were to rise another 8-10%. However, as of the quarter, it still maintains a trading level of below 156 NOK (as of the time of writing), and because of this, for September 2025, the company remains a "Buy".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a valuation target of 159 NOK/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a “Buy” rating as of September 2025. The company fulfills almost all of my fundamental criteria for investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arm: Strong AI Position, But Too High A Premium
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Arm Holdings (NASDAQ:ARM) is a tough investment. At its core, the business is not growing fast enough to get rewarded with a "hyper growth" label. In fact, I believe that after this upcoming quarter, its revenue growth rates are likely to decelerate as it comes up against more challenging comparables.

Meanwhile, the stock is still priced way higher than 150x forward free cash flow. This is just too much of an ask. Therefore, I remain neutral on ARM.

Quick Recap of My Position

Here you can read more about my previous views on Arm.

Arm's Near-Term Prospects

Arm builds the ''blueprints'' for computer chips that power pretty much everything—from smartphones to laptops and even cloud data centers.

Arm’s designs aim to deliver high performance while keeping power consumption low. That’s a huge deal in the age of AI, where workloads are exploding and efficiency is key.

Tech giants like Apple (AAPL), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), NVIDIA (NVDA), and AWS (AMZN) all rely on Arm’s platform to deliver faster and more energy-efficient devices and servers.

That said, smartphone demand has not been that strong, plus, growth comps get tougher after the next quarter.

At around 20% revenue growth, Arm could start looking less like a “hyper-growth” story.

On top of that, while Arm has carved out a strong niche in AI, it still faces heavyweight rivals like Intel’s (INTC) x86 architecture and AMD (AMD) in the data center, plus custom silicon efforts from the big cloud providers themselves.

So, it's not an easy setup for investors.

Revenue Growth Rates May Stabilize at 20%

Arm’s upcoming quarter will likely be its easiest comparable, which should make for a strong headline growth number. But beyond that, the next few quarters will be measured against much tougher comps. That sets the stage for revenue growth to settle closer to ~20% y/y, rather than the outsized figures some investors may be hoping for.

The problem is that ~20% y/y revenue growth isn’t the kind of number that convinces investors they’re looking at a true growth story. For a stock priced at such a premium, the market expects acceleration, not deceleration. Anything less than sustained 30%+ growth will struggle to justify Arm’s valuation and leaves little room for error in the narrative.

ARM Stock Valuation -- 204x Forward Free Cash Flow

As an Inflection investor, I avoid companies with flimsy balance sheets. Funny thing is, most investors don’t think twice about balance sheets—until the day it’s all anyone can think about. I prefer to be ahead of that curve.

On this front, Arm operates debt-free, which gives the business a lot of flexibility. Yes, admittedly, Arm holds under 2% of its market cap as cash, thereby it falls short of my desired criteria of 5% of my cash to market ratio.

Next, we know that Arm's free cash flow for the trailing twelve months was approximately $600 million, down from the roughly $700 million delivered in the prior twelve-month rolling period.

Accordingly, if we presume that over the next twelve months, Arm is able to increase its free cash flow by 25% y/y, this would get its free cash flow to around $750 million.

This is a steady bump from where it is right now, and yet, only a little higher than where it was in the past. In other words, assuming revenue grows by roughly 20% y/y, together with cost efficiencies and scale could make that 25% y/y FCF growth achievable.

This leaves the stock priced at approximately 204x forward free cash flow. However, we cut the story up, paying such a high free cash flow multiple leaves me with absolutely no margin of safety.

As a rule, I prefer an adjusted PEG ratio (using FCF instead of earnings) of under 3x. Right now, Arm sits closer to 10x. Maybe that drops to ~7x if investors get comfortable with 30%+ revenue growth next year—but that’s a big stretch.

Risk Factors to the Upside

The bulk of my concern is that the business is very highly valued, and yet, it delivers a lacklustre amount of free cash flow. If Arm were able to significantly improve its earnings to free cash flow potential, perhaps getting closer to $1 billion, this would dispel any bearish argument facing the stock.

Plus, as mentioned already, another risk factor towards the upside would be if Arm could grow its revenue line at a rate higher than 30% y/y. If investors could get confident that higher than 30% was the ''new normal, this would banish the bears in Arm's corner.

The Bottom Line

In conclusion, I find Arm to be too close to fully valued to leave me with much upside.

Think about it, the stock is already trading at more than 200x forward free cash flow, while revenue growth looks set to decelerate to perhaps around 20% y/y. Even at 25% y/y I would struggle to get bullish on Arm.

Yes, the business is debt-free. Also, it has a strong position in AI, but with free cash flow still hovering around, say $650 to 750 million and comps getting tougher, I just don’t see enough financial strength or growth momentum to justify this kind of premium.

Inflection investing is about backing companies where next year is likely to be better than this year. Not the reverse. No matter the story.

As such, I remain neutral on Arm as I have been for quite some time.

data.title: British American Tobacco: The Equity Story Has Been Exhausted (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I have been covering and carrying a long position in British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) since February 2024. At that time, when I built a notable stake in BTI, the case for me, as for an income-focused investor, seemed a 'no-brainer.' Apart from offering almost a double-digit yield, it ticked all of the necessary boxes for being considered a high and durable passive income pick, such as:

  • P/CF multiple at a low single-digit territory.
  • Investment-grade balance sheet.
  • Significant surplus in cash generation after the dividend distributions.
  • Relatively defensive business segment.
  • Cash-flow-focused management (i.e., no major/risky CapEx projects in the pipeline).
  • Stable dividend-paying track-record for more than 25 years in a row.

In a nutshell, by going long BTI, I was confident of two things: 1) receiving tangible and stress-free dividends, 2) sooner or later benefiting from valuation-driven (recovery process) price appreciation.

Since the publication of my original piece in early 2024, the stock has produced ~93% in total returns.

Going into 2025, I issued a follow-up article to address the significant stock price inflation aspect, and, effectively, to understand whether the thesis remains intact. The title of that piece says it all: British American Tobacco: My Largest Traditional Stock, This Is Why. After digesting the updated financials and putting that into the context of BTI's multiple and yield, my conclusion was that there was still some gas left in the tank for BTI to surprise investors to the upside.

The case has worked out smoothly as well with BTI registering a total return performance of ~43%.

While all of this is great, and, personally, I am happy to see that an income-focused stock has delivered and outperformed the booming markets such as the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), I have legitimate concerns about the future potential for BTI.

We have to understand that the lion's share of BTI's returns has stemmed from the recovery in previously very depressed levels. As a result of the higher valuation multiple, it is only natural that we see a much smaller yield now. Currently, BTI offers a dividend yield of 5.8%.

With this in mind, let me refresh my thesis on BTI.

Thesis update

I will start with a couple of facts covering the valuation front.

If we compare BTI's current valuations (using almost any metric) to its 5-year average levels, then we will notice 15% to 25% overvaluation, depending on the metric we choose. On a P/E basis, the difference lies closer to the upper range. In terms of the dividend yield, the situation does not look too expensive compared to the pre-COVID era, but relative to the past 5-year period, the yield is obviously not as attractive anymore

The valuation multiple that I like to use and what is usually applied in official valuation works by consultants and/or investment bankers is the EV/EBITDA. So, as of now, BTI trades at an EV/EBITDA of 9.5x, which is largely in line with quite many high-quality income-focused asset classes (securities).

I will give you two quick examples.

1. Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP) (BIPC) - an investment-grade, infrastructure-focused business offering ~5.5% yield and a similar level of annual dividend growth (at EV/EBITDA of 10.5x). See more details about the pick in my article here.

2. Energy Transfer (ET) - an investment-grade, midstream focused business offering a ~7.5% yield and a ~3% annual dividend growth (at EV/EBITDA of 9.2x). See more details about the pick in my article here.

I think that it is pretty clear that BTI does not anymore carry a deep bargain case that could be scooped up with an expectation of enjoying strong returns from some valuation multiple recovery process.

In my opinion, this does not mean that BTI is a bad investment or that investors should fire-sale the position. Instead, what it means is that we have to now be more focused on BTI's value (and risk) underlying drivers, as there is no longer an abnormal embedded margin of safety that would offset (neutralize) any struggles that BTI might experience in its financials. Namely, the bar for BTI to deliver strong returns has been set much higher than before.

The consensus estimates on BTI's EPS generation going forward indicate the same thing. For BTI, which operates in a mature and in some areas even declining business, achieving ~6% growth for two years in a row (2026 and 2027) is indeed a difficult task, to say the least.

Let's unpack this assumption a bit.

Over the past 5-year period, BTI has managed to expand EBITDA by ~4.5%, which is less than what has been baked into the cake for 2026 and 2027 (for EPS). Historical 5-year EPS growth has landed at roughly the same level of ~4.6%. So, the current expectation means that BTI has to find additional avenues through which to not only maintain the tempo but also enhance it by a rate that is actually quite tangible for any mature value business.

In my opinion, it will be extremely difficult for BTI to achieve this.

Here is why.

A large part of the realized EPS growth has stemmed from aggressive buybacks, which have been fueled by some extraordinary items like divestitures and equity stake monetization in ICT. For example, relatively recently BTI sold a decent chunk of ICT, where the proceeds were directed towards bumping up the buyback program from ~$260 million to $1.5 billion (I have converted from pounds to USD). Some part of these proceeds was also parked at the balance sheet level in order to de-leverage the business.

We have to appreciate that the selected equity stake monetizations are inherently one-offs, which cannot happen again and again going forward. In practice, BTI will have to find fresh EBITDA sources to both replace the one-off item drivers and secure incremental growth.

Looking at the current financials, the story does not look that encouraging in the context of meeting the aforementioned targets. The H1 results indicate that the BTI as a group has managed to grow the top line by only 1%. On the operating cash flow level, the growth has been better, but not even close to what is expected (at ~2%).

My key concerns lie in the conventional cigarette segment, which has remained under pressure for many years in a row, and shows no signs of stopping. For example, in H1, the YTD sales were down by ~11%. And let's not forget that this particular segment accounts for a notable part of BTI's total EBITDA generation.

Granted, there are new categories, which are offsetting the negatives associated with combustibles, but the question is how durable the new category trend is. In H1 the vapor segment had actually declined. In heated products, the gross volume share for BIT was down by 0.9% in top markets.

Furthermore, as a prudent investor, I would operate with an assumption that BTI's core business will continue to face structural headwinds from increased taxation, restrictions, and mushrooming illegal product sales. For example, it is hard to imagine how governments across the world would not explore ways to boost revenues from such a populistic area like tobacco taxation (especially, given the mounting deficits).

The bottom line

In nutshell, BTI is a hold for me, and not a buy.

There are two major reasons why I have stopped adding more to BTI's position (i.e., canceled the DRIP plan).

First, the dividend yield of 5.8% is not that attractive anymore, especially compared to other durable and sound alternatives such as ET and BIP, which yield the same or even more and at the same time offer better growth prospects (and inherently more defensive business models).

Second, the current multiple is underpinned by too optimistic growth assumptions. In my humble opinion, in the best case, BTI could deliver on the "priced in" EPS trajectory, which would basically justify a somewhat flat share price. Yet, as outlined in the article above, my base assumption is that BTI falls short of the EPS consensus growth figures, which, in turn, would give a solid basis for the market to reprice the stock lower.

data.title: Costco: Great Business, Still A Hold On Valuation
data.description:
When I last looked at Costco Wholesale Corporation (NASDAQ:COST) in June, I called it a Hold at about $999 a share. My main concern was the high price, not the company itself. Since then, the stock has slipped to around $943, down about 5.6 percent, even as the company delivered another strong quarter. To me, that shows the market is already aware of how expensive the stock is and that price, not performance, is the main hurdle.

I still feel neutral here. Costco’s FY25 results showed revenue growth of 8 percent and solid profit gains, yet at more than 52 times forward earnings, the shares look stretched. The case for owning it is clear: dependable membership income, growth in online sales and steady global expansion. The other side is also clear: slowing comps, stronger competition from Aldi and Walmart Inc. (WMT) and the possibility that investors stop paying any price for quality.

In short, Costco is still a fantastic business with a strong balance sheet but the stock does not offer much room for upside. At this level, I see it moving sideways more than higher. My view is that Costco is a Hold and that investors should wait for a pullback closer to $850 to $900 before adding.

Memberships and Digital Growth Power the Model

Costco’s model is simple and very effective. Membership fees cover much of the profit base, while thin margins and massive sales volume keep customers loyal. Membership income in FY25 grew more than 13 percent to $5.3 billion and the number of paid members rose to 81 million. Renewal rates stayed close to 90 percent around the world and executive members drove nearly three-quarters of sales. These numbers underline just how steady and reliable Costco’s business is, even when people are watching their budgets.

On top of that, the company keeps building its reach. Costco ended FY25 with 914 warehouses and already plans 35 more in FY26. Its digital side is also picking up momentum. Website traffic was up 27 percent and online sales rose more than 13 percent. Investments in logistics and site design are starting to pay off, making Costco more appealing to people who shop online first.

In my view, Costco’s key strengths are its membership model, the Kirkland private label and a steady plan for expansion. The real question is not whether Costco can keep performing but whether the current stock price leaves enough room for returns.

Market Leadership Faces New Competitive Pressures

Costco is the clear leader in the warehouse club space. BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (BJ) and Sam’s Club are still around, but they do not have the same level of trust or scale. In today’s environment, where families are stretched by food and living costs, Costco’s value pitch is even stronger.

At the same time, rivals are stepping up. Aldi is pushing hard in the United States and refreshing its private-label lines. Walmart, Target Corporation (TGT) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) are also trying to win more of the everyday basket. Even though they cannot fully copy Costco’s formula, they can chip away at market share.

So while Costco still has the advantage, competition is getting tougher. Over time, that may make it harder for Costco to keep posting the same strong comparable sales.

Solid Financial Results Backed by Membership Strength

Costco’s FY25 revenue climbed to nearly $270 billion, up just over 8 percent, while net income grew 10 percent to a little more than $8 billion. Earnings per share were $18.21, up from $16.56 the year before. Margins held steady, with gross margin improving slightly and EBIT margin sitting around 3.7 percent. Cash flow was very strong, rising 17 percent to $13.3 billion, and the company finished the year with more than $14 billion in cash compared to $5.7 billion in long-term debt.

Membership remains Costco’s most powerful driver. Paid members increased more than 6 percent and membership fees made up almost 2 percent of revenue while contributing far more than that to profit. Renewal rates above 90 percent in the United States and Canada show the stickiness of the model.

Looking forward, analysts expect EPS to rise to about $20.5 in FY26 and $22.2 in FY27, which means growth of around 9 to 10 percent a year. That is solid but it is not enough to justify the current valuation.

To me, the story is simple: Costco is cash-rich, efficient and dependable. The challenge is that earnings growth in the high single digits does not match a price tag above 50 times forward earnings.

Valuation Leaves Little Margin for Safety

At its current price near $943, Costco trades at roughly 52 times forward earnings and about 33 times EV to EBITDA. Walmart trades at about 20 times that multiple and Target trades even lower. The PEG ratio sits above 5, which points to overvaluation.

Even a generous discounted cash flow model, using 8 percent long-term earnings growth and a 7 percent discount rate, lands at a fair value of around $850. That means the stock is priced for perfection, with no real margin of safety.

From my perspective, Costco does deserve a premium but the gap between price and growth is too wide. The stock is already reflecting the best possible outcome. If the multiple comes down, even a little, the shares could easily lose ground.

Investor Sentiment Turns More Cautious

Costco continues to enjoy positive headlines but the tone is becoming more cautious. The company’s recent quarter beat expectations, yet slowing comps have caught the attention of analysts. Total adjusted comparable sales were up 6.4 percent, which is good but not as strong as earlier runs.

News coverage has also included less flattering items, like recalls of Prosecco bottles, lawsuits such as the one from Lululemon and chatter about tariffs. None of these alone threatens the company but together they remind investors that even Costco faces bumps.

The feeling in the market is still mostly upbeat, yet it is less relaxed than before. With expectations already so high, even small disappointments could hit the stock harder than they should.

Key Risks Are More About Valuation Than Business Model

The biggest risk for Costco is a drop in valuation multiples. If investors reset their willingness to pay for quality and the stock falls from 52 times earnings to something closer to 35 or 40, that would mean a quick 20 to 30 percent decline, even if the business itself stays on track. Slower comparable sales are another clear risk. If comps continue to cool, growth in earnings could stall. Competition is another piece of the puzzle, as Aldi, Walmart and online-first retailers work harder to capture customers. Legal and regulatory challenges, such as lawsuits and tariffs, add another layer of uncertainty. And although Costco tends to hold up well when the economy weakens, a sharp slowdown would still affect its discretionary categories.

In my view, the danger is not whether Costco can execute but whether the market keeps rewarding it with a sky-high multiple.

Steady Growth Outlook Without Clear Upside Catalyst

Most forecasts show Costco growing earnings by 9 to 10 percent annually through FY28. Warehouse growth and a strong online presence should support this path. Membership growth will continue to anchor the company’s stability.

The issue is that the growth trend looks steady, not accelerating. That makes it harder to see a clear catalyst for a re-rating at today’s valuation. Unless the international segment takes off more quickly or online sales become a higher-margin driver, the stock may simply move sideways.

So, my outlook is that Costco will keep growing at a steady pace but not in a way that justifies a price of more than 50 times earnings.

Bear, Base and Bull Paths for the Stock

In a bear case, the shares could fall to around $750 if multiples shrink and comps weaken further. In a base case, the stock would hover near $900 as Costco delivers steady growth but valuations adjust downward. In a bull case, the shares might reach $1,100 if growth continues smoothly, international expansion speeds up and the market decides to keep paying for quality without pause.

To me, the most likely outcome is somewhere between the base and bear scenarios, which means downside risk is greater than the chance of a sharp upside.

Final View: A Great Company but Not a Great Entry Price

I see Costco as one of the strongest companies in retail. Its balance sheet is healthy, its members are loyal and its growth model is proven. What gives me pause is not the business but the stock’s price.

At about 52 times forward earnings, Costco’s shares already reflect the best possible version of the story. While the company will likely keep growing, the stock may not deliver strong returns from here. For that reason, I keep my Hold rating. Owners of the stock should feel comfortable keeping it but they should lower their expectations for near-term gains. Those looking to buy should wait for a dip into the $850 to $900 range, where the tradeoff would look more appealing.

data.title: It's Time For Nvidia Stock To Go Higher Again
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Since my previous earnings preview article on NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) (TSX:NVDA:CA), which came out a few days before the firm reported late last month for its fiscal Q2 2026, I see that the stock price basically went nowhere, while the market generally was in positive territory for the same period:

I think this is a temporary thing, as some short-term thinkers and speculators were "selling on the news" and trimming their long positions. In reality, the prospects of Nvidia seem to stay there, and the stock now looks even more attractive given the lagging nature of the stock price amid once again better-than-expected earnings for the quarter. The future looks brighter for NVDA's EPS now, thanks to Q2 and all the updates that flooded the market, so I think it's time for NVDA to go higher from here. That's why I'm reiterating my Buy rating on NVDA today.

Why Do I Think So?

I expected yet another beat from Nvidia in my August earnings preview article, and we got it: Nvidia's Q2 revenue of $46.74 billion (+56% YoY) and adjusted EPS of $1.05 (+54% YoY) beat the consensus estimates by 1.49% and 4.05%, respectively. In my view, most investors have gotten used to seeing Nvidia beat Wall Street's estimates by 5-6% quarterly, so this time the double-beat didn't impress them - hence the stock's negative price action, which almost reached -10% at some point after the Q2 release:

Now NVDA is gradually recovering from the post-earnings reaction, and I think it will eventually go even higher than its ATHs.

The main reason for my conviction flows from the demand side: the global AI infrastructure spending by the end of the decade should amount to $3-4 trillion, according to CEO Jensen Huang's commentary on the Q2 call, so the gradual total addressable market, or TAM, expansion should naturally push this niche near-monopolist and leader to higher highs. And I'd get the point of those who question the CEO's commentary if there were no supporting estimates from third parties. But there are plenty of both direct and indirect signs that he's right.

For example, according to BofA's recent report (proprietary source, September 2025), the global data center market will be growing at a CAGR of 24% up to 2028, and if we sum up the total spending for the 2026-2028 period alone, we'll get ~$2.4 trillion. And it's just 3 years of CapEx, so even with 0% growth beyond 2028, the next 5 years' consolidated spending will be close to $4.3 trillion (up to 2030, inclusive), if my math is right.

NVDA's DC segment brings the lion's share of the firm's sales, and it went up by 56% YoY in Q2 thanks to the faster-than-initially-assumed transition to the Blackwell architecture. The market's adoption of AI agents, based on available stats, is already pretty solid, with ~"78% of organizations already using AI in some form, and 85% having adopted agents in at least one workflow":

I'm sure that in most of those cases, it's just 1 or a maximum of 2 layers of the workflow that are actually automated by AI because it's still too complicated a process. But these AI agents and their role grow rapidly in all industries, and it's just getting hard to deny. And with greater needs comes greater GPU capacity requirements. That's why OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said recently that even with Nvidia's massive $100 billion investment, there should be more spending ahead, and it's totally normal.

It's a smart move from Nvidia to invest this much into its prospective biggest customer, as it adds some form of revenue protection in the future. If they help to deploy at least 10 gigawatts of AI data center capacity, as the reports suggest, Nvidia's forward EPS visibility should only improve from this, in my understanding. BofA analysts concur, estimating that this partnership alone could generate about $300-500 billion in revenue for Nvidia over time:

The partnership includes a letter of intent for NVDA to be involved in at least 10 GW of systems, starting in 2H26 with Vera Rubin, which we estimate could be [roughly] $300-$500B in revenue over time, or roughly a 3x-5x return on investment,” Bank of America analysts, led by Vivek Arya, wrote in a note to clients. “Second, and perhaps more importantly, OpenAI will work with NVDA as a preferred strategic compute and networking partner. While no share is specified, the agreement on surface raises the competitive risks for other vendors including AVGO and AMD.

Source: Seeking Alpha News [September 22, 2025].

Nvidia's Hopper-Blackwell transition was the driver of great top and bottom line expansion in the past quarters, and now, with Blackwell Ultra (GB300) ramping at full speed, the firm's next platform (Rubin) is already in the fabrication stage at TSMC (TSM), and it should come online in 2H 2026 (so effectively, it's calendar 2H 2025). This partially explains the recent earnings revisions that took place beyond just Q3 2026 (mostly we see them for Q1/Q4 FY2028, according to Seeking Alpha):

So, on a net-net basis, nothing bad happened to Nvidia in the past few weeks, and especially there are no signs of real business deterioration in its Q2 report. However, as I noted earlier, the stock dropped by almost 10% after the Q2 came out, and although the price almost recovered to its pre-Q2 values, the gap between the valuation and fundamentals is still there.

I mean, on an EV/EBITDA basis, NVDA might look pricey trading at 33.73x next year's EBITDA. But given the recent revisions, which were made not only for EPS but for EBITDA estimates as well, we see that in 2-3 years from now, NVDA's forward EV/EBITDA drops to 21-24x. So, the stock is trading at its lowest relative valuation right now, taking forward multiples as the key metrics for analysis.

This formed gap between valuation and the path of Nvidia's key financials in Q2 doesn't look fair, so I think it gives NVDA bulls some reasonable hope to expect the price to push higher from here.

I'm not the only analyst on the Street seeing the undervaluation. According to Argus Research's post-Q2 update, the undervaluation may be close to 97% (if we take their DFCF valuation model's output):

Relative to the peer group, NVDA trades at premiums on P/E, price/sales, and EV/EBITDA, and at a discount on PEG. Peer-indicated value of about $110 is rising slightly. Nvidia has become a much-faster-growing company than it was over the preceding five years, and in our view deserves to trade at very rich premiums to peers and to its own historical valuations.

On our forward-looking DFCF valuation, NVDA is valued in the $350s. Our blended valuation is around $305, well above current prices.

Source: Argus Research (proprietary source, August 2025)

But I wouldn't be this bullish in the medium term.

The relative consensus beating in Q1/Q2 does show some slowdown, so it's more reasonable to assume only a slight premium on the existing consensus. I think 5% is fair, and it would take into account the odds of further upward revisions. So, at 35x forward earnings, which is fair for Nvidia given its historical valuation range and its business moat, the price target for next year arrives at ~$233/share. So, the upside potential is close to 31%, and it justifies a Buy rating confirmation from my end.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

As I noted in my August article, Nvidia bulls should be critical about some risks surrounding the stock. For example, the continued rise in Advanced Micro Devices' (AMD) role in the GPU market, and even if AMD only holds 4-5% of the GPU market (according to some sources), it can be much higher than that in the next 2-5 years because AMD keeps rolling out new offerings. So, if Nvidia starts to lose big on its dominance, it can start a pricing war, which can lead to lower margins and hence less valuation premium over time. This makes my 35x earnings multiple projection less sustainable.

My whole valuation thesis and findings are quite risky, I admit it. Assuming no premium added to the current FY2027 EPS consensus estimate and putting only 30x earnings on them, I get a price target of only $190.2/share, which gives me an upside potential of less than 7% to the current stock price.

EPS FY2027 * No Premium (factor = 1) * P/E multiple = PT

$6.34 * 1 * 30x = $190.2/share.

At a lower multiple, say, 25x earnings, NVDA's price target would drop to only $158.5/share, and then we'd have a downside potential of almost 11%.

The Bottom Line

I admit that risks are high with following every new ATH that Nvidia breaks in terms of its stock price action, but I think that after their Q2 report, the subsequent price action decoupled from fundamentals. Also, the recent news that Nvidia may invest a massive $100 billion sum into OpenAI and thus secure its own earnings visibility for the next few years (at least) is making me even more bullish on the GPU maker's prospects.

Their top line is projected to compound by a CAGR of over 16% in the next 5 years, according to the current consensus, and it's very likely to drive up the stock's valuation, this way eventually smoothing out the gap that I identified.

With all that in mind, I decided to leave my Buy rating unchanged. It's probably time for Nvidia to update its ATHs soon.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Can Hyperliquid See Off Threat From Dark Pool DEXs?
data.description:
Introduction

The hype on perpetual futures decentralised exchanges (DEXs) is heating up, thanks in part to Hyperliquid (HYPE-USD), whose price has surged more than 350% since July 4.

And with hype comes more interest, which is why more platforms are looking to get in the game early, before the market gets out of reach.

Decentralised crypto exchanges experienced a slower adoption compared to their centralised counterparts like Binance.

Unlike centralised exchange platforms, DEXs operate on a peer-to-peer mechanism in an attempt to limit the number of intermediaries involved in the market.

And while they have been around for just about as long as their centralised peers, the likes of Binance have continued to dominate the industry.

That has changed recently, as evidenced by the open interest in Bitcoin Futures products across top trading platforms. Hyperliquid has emerged as one of the top platforms for perpetual Bitcoin swaps, with $2.4 billion in open interest.

However, its position is now being challenged by the likes of Aster, EdgeX, and Lighter.

Over the past 24 hours, BNB Chain-based Aster saw its transaction volume skyrocket to a whopping $25 billion, more than double Hyperliquid’s $10 billion over the same timeframe. EdgeX’s $8 billion and Lighter’s $6 billion are still below the leading perpetual DEX platform, but not far off, according to DeFiLlama data.

Overall, perpetual DEX trading volume across platforms tracked by DeFiLlama has spiked recently, to hit a new daily high of about $52 billion. This illustrates the increasing interest in the sector, especially since the turn of the year.

Why are dark pool DEXs gaining popularity?

Aster’s recent transaction volume spike is not by accident. Backed by YZi Labs, the platform has been hyped up, including by top crypto people like Chengpeng Zhao.

In a recent post on the X platform, Zhao wrote, “Aster cracks me up, leverage me fully (pun intended). Well played.” The post was accompanied by a repost of Aster’s announcement that users can trade $HYPE token, Hyperliquid’s native token, with up to 300% leverage.

While Aster is a direct competitor of Hyperliquid, it is a slightly different kind of decentralised exchange platform.

Unlike Hyperliquid, where transactions are completely transparent, Aster uses Dark Pools, which are trading avenues that allow large trades to happen privately without immediately appearing on the public blockchain.

It is the same trade mechanism applied by institutional investors like hedge funds in traditional financial markets to avoid causing sharp market swings.

One of the benefits of using Dark Pools in decentralised exchanges is the ability to reduce slippage and prevent sophisticated investors from front-running trades.

While platforms like Hyperliquid, which make trade details immediately available on the order book data flow, remain popular with most traders, large investors, who put in millions of dollars per position, would be better protected if order details were kept secret until the transaction is completed.

This ensures sophisticated investors who target large positions to front-run and arbitrage for profits do not get a chance.

Grvt co-founder and CEO Hong Yea shares a similar view, according to a recent Forbes article. "We've seen those cases where they do get hunted," Yea told Forbes contributor Boaz Sobrado, referring to large traders whose positions become targets for arbitrage opportunities. "Many of those folks who take high leverage on very large positions—let's say $50 million—they don't want to be hunted."

Yea’s perpetual futures DEX leverages zero-knowledge proofs to keep trade details hidden from the transaction orderbook until they are finalised. And like Aster, his company seems to be benefiting from the rising popularity of dark pools, with its 7-day transaction volume crossing $2 billion on Thursday, according to Yea’s post on the X platform.

The interest in dark pool DEXs is further illustrated by venture capital firms, which continue to inject capital into the sector. More recently, Grvt raised $19 million in a Series A round led by enterprise-grade blockchain network ZKsync, which invested $14 million.

Can Hyperliquid bounce back from dark pool DEXs' pressure?

Aster has overtaken Hyperliquid in short-term transaction volume. However, in the longer time frame, the leading decentralised exchange platform, which has seen its market share fall to about 38% from 71% in May, continues to dominate numbers, with more than double Aster’s 30-day transaction volume of $115 billion versus its figure of $284 billion, according to DeFiLlama data.

But it's not just the volume; looking at other metrics like open interest, Hyperliquid’s figure of $12.66 billion is about ten times Aster’s $1.267 billion. Hyperliquid also boasts more than three times Aster's total value locked, with $6.4 billion versus $2.1 billion.

The point here is that Hyperliquid has used its market dominance to cement its financial position, which allows it the flexibility to absorb certain market shocks as those it is experiencing due to pressure from Aster, Lighter, EdgeX, Grvt, and others.

Should you buy HYPE after the pullback?

Hyperliquid’s native token HYPE has pulled back significantly amid selling pressure after ceding ground to Aster and others. The HYPE price has fallen from a high of about $58.92 on September 18 to trade at $42.80, reflecting a decline of approximately 27%. Subsequently, HYPE has erased all gains made during the preceding two-week period.

Given Hyperliquid’s token-holder value-boosting incentives, which include using a majority of fees generated to buy back HYPE, versus the pressure the company is facing from rivals, that is still not enough to support a buy argument. However, it does provide investors with the confidence to hold for now.

The company has also launched a GENIUS Act-compliant stablecoin, USDH, further opening the door to institutions that may seek compliant-ready infrastructure. The USDH stablecoin also reduces Hyperliquid’s overreliance on third-party stablecoins like the USDT and USDC.

The dark pool threat is real

While Hyperliquid’s strong data and regulatory-compliant products suggest it is well-positioned in the market to battle the storm, the threat of dark pool DEXs can not be ignored. Even upcoming players like Grvt have seen their transaction volume double within a week, further illustrating the reception dark pool DEXs are getting in the market.

Aster, which boasts backing from YZi Labs, is quickly eating into Hyperliquid’s perpetual futures DEXs market share, and with Zhao relentlessly drumming up its campaign, it may take time before the buzz fizzles out. That means there could be more downward movement in HYPE before the next rebound.

Therefore, it may be wise to hold HYPE while keeping a close eye on how the perpetual futures exchange battle unfolds amid the buzz surrounding dark pools.

data.title: Italian Confidence Data Broadly Stable In September
data.description:
By Paolo Pizzoli

Small improvement in consumer confidence masks uncertainty

Private consumption was the most disappointing demand component in the second quarter. Despite a further recovery in household purchasing power - supported by inflation below 2%, resilient employment, and solid wage growth - spending remained flat. Confidence data over the summer has pointed towards stabilisation, and today’s small improvement in sentiment does not mark any substantial change. Consumers seem less worried about economic developments, but this has not eased their concerns about future unemployment. Nevertheless, they continue to signal a growing opportunity to purchase durable goods. The jury is still out as to whether consumption will once again become a driver of growth in the third quarter.

Stabilisation is the name of the game in manufacturing

Confidence remains stable in the manufacturing segment, supported by an improvement among producers of intermediate and investment goods, despite a decline among consumer goods manufacturers. Forward-looking indicators suggest no major shifts ahead, as a small improvement in orders is offset by increased inventories and a decline in expected production. Current stock levels appear sufficient to meet expected demand, reducing the need to ramp up production. The manufacturing sector seems to have exited its contraction phase, although a clear recovery path has yet to emerge.

Stability in construction confidence hides divergence among components

On the construction front, the recent pattern seems to be holding: confidence has improved in the construction of infrastructure and has declined among residential builders. Nothing new here, as this reflects the approaching 2026 deadline for projects under the National Recovery and Resilience Plan and the exhaustion of the Superbonus effect. The ultimate contribution of the construction sector to third-quarter GDP growth looks uncertain right now, notwithstanding encouraging signals from July production data.

Confidence in services creeps up

The small improvement in services confidence offers no clear-cut insights, though it appears to be driven by business-related services. More puzzling is the signal from tourism-related services, where respondents report a sharp decline in current orders alongside an improvement in expected orders. Services have been a key pillar of support for the supply side of the Italian economy throughout the post-Covid recovery. That still seems to be the case - albeit with signs of fatigue.

Modest growth still in sight in the third quarter

Overall, today’s data remains consistent with a very modest economic rebound in the third quarter. This is likely driven by a smaller drag from net exports and continued investment-led growth, with a possible minor contribution from consumption, which remains uncertain. We therefore confirm our annual GDP growth forecast of 0.5% for 2025.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post
